---
title: "L01 Review"
subtitle: "Data Science 3 with R (STAT 301-3)"
author: "ARUNDHATI DOLE"
pagetitle: "L01 ARUNDHATI DOLE"
date: today

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: false
    link-external-newwindow: true

execute:
  warning: false

from: markdown+emoji
reference-location: margin
citation-location: margin   
---

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Github Repo Link

To link to your github **repo**sitory, appropriately edit the example link below. Meaning replace `https://your-github-repo-url` with your github repo url. Suggest verifying the link works before submitting.

[Arundhati's Github Link](https://github.com/stat301-3-2026-spring/l01-review-arundhatidole2026-1)
:::

## Overview

The goal of this lab is to review machine learning/predictive modeling vocabulary and concepts covered in Data Science 2 with R (STAT 301-2).

## Questions

When completing the following questions ensure your solutions are neatly formatted and clearly indicated. Consider including diagrams/images in some of your solutions, if it helps to make things easier to describe/discuss.

### Question 1

Provide a brief outline/overview of the steps involved in a supervised machine learning process. Could provide this as a bulleted list. 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

First, decide whether this is a regression or classification problem. 
Divide the data set into training and testing sets.
Set up folds and repeats. 
Then create recipes for your models. 
Define and fit the appropriate models for the problem, also making sure to tune the models. 
Use a set of models which is appropriate for the problem, then select the model with the best metrics, such as the highest accuracy or lowest rmse. 
Test the testing data with the best model.
Build a confusion matrix to analyze the final results. 

:::

### Question 2

Explain the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Unsupervised models are not given a target variable, use unlabelled data, and may use techniques such as clustering, association, or dimensionality reduction. 
On the other hand, a supervised model is given more guidance, usually using pre-categorized data and either classification or regression models. 

:::

### Question 3 

In general, we can classify a model by its purpose into 1 of the 3 categories below. Provide a brief description of the goals of these model classes.

#### Descriptive Models 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Descriptive models provide a summary of certain characteristics of the data, like the average, the range, or the spread of the data. These types of models emphasize some trend or artifact of data. An example might be analyzing the statistics behind test scores in a class.

:::

#### Inferential Models

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

An inferential model produces a decision for a research question or tests a hypothesis, and depend on underlying assumptions. For example, you may be looking at which factors play the most role in determining test scores, whether that is hours of sleep, hours spent studying, hours spent exercising e.t.c. 

:::

#### Predictive Models

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Predictive models produce the most accurate prediction possible for new data, such as predicting what grade each student will receive on the test. 

:::

### Question 4 

We can further describe/classify predictive models by how they were derived or developed as being either mechanistic or empirically driven. 

#### Part (a)

What does it mean to be a mechanistic model?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

A mechanistic model has a definite equation, like Y = B0 + B1X1 + B2X2 + ... These models do not require a lot of data and are easier to interpret. However, they have limited complexity, and the equation may not be a good fit. 

:::

#### Part (b)

What does it mean to be an empirically driven model?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

An empirical model has no assumptions about the function f(x). These tend to be more flexible and have better predictive performance. However, they take longer and are at a higher risk of overfitting. 

:::

#### Part (c)

How does the mechanistic and empirically driven model terminology relate to the parametric and nonparametric model terminology? 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

"Mechanistic models" are another term for parametric models, and "empirically driven models" are another term for nonparametric models. 

:::

#### Part (d)

In general, is a mechanistic or empirically driven model easier to interpret? Explain.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

A mechanistic model is easier to interpret, since it follows a known equation. 

:::

#### Part (e)

How does mechanistic and empirically driven model terminology relate to the idea of model flexibility? That is, which would be more or less flexible than the other.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Empirically driven models usually offer more flexibility, since it does not assume a predefined function, and are more adaptable. Examples include decision trees, k-nearest neighbors, and spline regression. 

:::

#### Part (f)

Describe the bias-variance trade-off when considering the use of a mechanistic or empirically driven model. 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 5 

Explain the difference between a regression and classification machine learning (ML) problems.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

A classification aims to sort observations into different categories, while a regression problem predicts a certain number. For instance, a regression problem might predict what will house prices be in ten years, while a classification problem might predict whether it will be warm or cold tomorrow. 

:::

### Question 6 

When splitting the data, why is it useful to stratify by the outcome/target variable? 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

It is useful to stratify by the target level because you want to make sure that you have a random mix of this variable in both the training and testing set. Otherwise, your results might be biased. The trainig set tests different model fits, tunes model hyperparamters, and looks at various feature engineering strategies. On the other hand, the test data set is solely used for evaluating the performance of the model. 

:::

### Question 7 

Briefly describe how v-fold cross validation with repeats is used to estimate test RMSE. Also provide an explanation of why we use it. 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution



:::

### Question 8

When might we use a bootstrap resampling procedure instead of v-fold cross validation to estimate test RMSE?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 9 

Briefly describe model tuning and why we use it.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 10 

What are two common performance metrics when dealing with a regression ML problem?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

What are two common performance metrics when dealing with a classification ML problem?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 11

Classify each question/problem below as either prediction or inferential. Explain your reasoning for each.

A company with a subscription based service (for example Netflix, Disney+, New York Times, etc) has data concerning customer interactions with a them, including features like the number of customer service calls, quality of service calls, subscription length, engagement with the service, discounted service, etc. They are interested in two questions:

1. Does a high number of service calls impact a customer's likelihood of churn in the next month (leave the company/drop the service)?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

2. How likely is it that a customer will churn in the next month (leave the company/drop the service)?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::
